narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 59% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt—a threshold that leads to rejection to ensure incoming students are positioned for success. As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and middle-family income background, you stood out positively in some areas compared to typical applicants, whose average undergrad GPA is 3.21, but other elements tipped the scales toward concern. The model's decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on this specific prediction.

The most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the average of around 5.5 and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as it signals a dip in performance relative to peers that the model associates with exam struggles. Next, your undergraduate GPA provided a counterbalance, pulling the prediction toward success more than for most applicants since it's above the typical 3.21 average, showing your capacity for strong academic work earlier on. Your LSAT score of 26, however, which is well below the average of 36.7, further nudged the outlook toward failure, reinforcing doubts about standardized test readiness for the bar.

Overall, these factors placed you below the profiles of students typically predicted to pass, where higher decile rankings and LSAT scores dominate. While your full-time enrollment intent aligns with the vast majority of applicants, it didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted study or additional coursework, and reapplying once you've addressed these gaps—many students in similar situations succeed on a second try with focused improvements.
